title: Debt sustainability analysis for euro area sovereigns: a methodological framework
summary: This ECB Occasional Paper introduces a comprehensive debt sustainability analysis (DSA) framework for euro area sovereigns, consisting of three building blocks: deterministic DSA, stochastic DSA, and other indicators capturing liquidity and solvency risks. The framework is designed to assess fiscal vulnerabilities and provide policy warnings, but is a methodological reference document rather than a current market signal.
key_points: The DSA framework comprises three main building blocks: deterministic DSA (benchmark and narrative shock scenarios over 10-year horizon), stochastic DSA (probabilistic approach to debt sustainability), and other relevant indicators for liquidity/solvency risks
Deterministic DSA evaluates debt projections based on three criteria: debt level, debt dynamics, and feasibility of sustaining primary surpluses
Stochastic DSA provides probability assessments for debt levels exceeding thresholds, probability of debt not stabilizing, and dispersion of simulated debt paths
Short-run liquidity risks include net financing needs and a composite index of ease of refinancing
Medium to longer-run risks encompass public debt maturity structure, contingent liabilities scope, net financial position, and governance/political risk
The framework can be summarized in a heat map format for overall debt sustainability risk assessment
why_it_matters: This paper provides the methodological foundation for ECB's sovereign debt sustainability assessments. While not a current market catalyst, it establishes the analytical framework that informs ECB fiscal surveillance under the Stability and Growth Pact and guides assessments of euro area sovereign vulnerabilities.
macro_context: Debt sustainability analysis is relevant to euro area sovereign bond markets and fiscal policy surveillance. The framework helps identify countries at risk of debt instability, which can affect sovereign spreads and ECB policy responses. However, this 2017 methodological paper does not constitute a current macro catalyst.
